summary:
The Kura-Araxes culture, also known as the Early Transcaucasian culture, was a Chalcolithic to early Bronze Age culture centered in the South Caucasus — modern Georgia, Armenia, and Azerbaijan — that flourished approximately 3500–2000 BCE. Named after the Kura and Araxes rivers of the region, the culture is recognized by its distinctive handmade black and red burnished pottery, circular or rectangular mud-brick dwellings arranged around a central hearth, and a strongly pastoral economy based on cattle, sheep, and goat herding.

The Kura-Araxes people were remarkably mobile, and their material culture spread across a vast area encompassing Anatolia, the northern Levant, Iran, and even parts of Mesopotamia during the late fourth millennium BCE. This expansion — likely driven by transhumant pastoralism and trade — represents one of the earliest large-scale cultural dispersals in the ancient Near East. Sites associated with the culture show evidence of copper smelting, and the Kura-Araxes metallurgical tradition may have contributed to the spread of bronze technology across the wider region.
